planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Bobo's dwarf stature (2.5–3 feet) makes it proportionate for foundation plantings, containers, and small residential borders where larger panicle hydrangeas would be overwhelming.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Bobo is the most compact of the widely available panicle hydrangea cultivars — a dwarf selection staying just 2.5–3 feet tall and wide while producing flower panicles nearly as large as those of full-size cultivars like Limelight. This unusually large flower-to-plant-size ratio gives Bobo a heavily blooming, almost top-heavy appearance at peak bloom that creates a dramatic visual impact from a very compact plant. The large, cone-shaped flower panicles open pure white in mid-summer and transition through blush-pink to dusty rose as fall approaches — a 3–4 month evolving color display. Bobo's compact, mounding habit and relatively sturdy stems that hold the large flower heads erect without flopping make it one of the most practically useful panicle hydrangea introductions for residential landscape use. Like all Hydrangea paniculata cultivars, Bobo blooms on new wood and is pruned hard in late winter (to 12 inches or shorter) to encourage vigorous new growth and maximum flower production. This new-wood blooming habit means the timing of pruning is not critical — unlike the French hydrangeas that must be pruned at precisely the right time or lose the season's bloom. In zone 9, afternoon shade helps maintain flower color and extend the display season through the intense summer heat.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 5.5–7.0) soil in partial shade when young; Susquehanna pairs effectively with Shenandoah or Wells for cross-pollination, as the Peterson pawpaws were selected to maximize cross-productivity with one another.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade when young, transitioning to full sun at fruiting age; Susquehanna is one of the flagship 'Peterson Pawpaws' — the group of named cultivars selected and introduced by Neal Peterson from decades of pawpaw breeding aimed at developing the largest, sweetest, and most productive cultivars available. Susquehanna produces some of the largest individual pawpaw fruits of any named cultivar, with exceptional fruits reaching 12–16 ounces — two to three times the average size of a good seedling-grown pawpaw — on a productive, healthy tree. The extraordinarily large fruit size, excellent sweet flavor with rich banana-custard-vanilla character, and low seed-to-flesh ratio (maximum edible flesh per fruit) make Susquehanna the top choice among pawpaw enthusiasts seeking maximum yield from a home orchard planting. Cross-pollination from Shenandoah, Wells, or another unrelated cultivar is required for reliable production — plant two or more different named cultivars within 50 feet. Because pawpaw flowers have an unusual pollination mechanism (receptive to pollen only briefly, with fly/beetle pollinators), hand-pollination using a small brush to transfer pollen between flowers at different stages significantly improves fruit set. In zone 9, pawpaws perform in the northern portions of the zone; deep South heat and insufficient winter chilling can limit production in zones 9b and warmer.
sources: Kentucky State University Pawpaw Research Program

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, average to lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Autumn Joy declines in rich, wet soil — excessive fertility and moisture cause floppy stems and reduced flowering; plant in lean, well-drained conditions for the most compact, upright habit.

directions: Plant in well-drained, average to lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Autumn Joy (the trade name for Hylotelephium telephium 'Herbstfreude', a hybrid of German origin) is one of the most widely planted and most beloved perennials in American horticulture — valued for its exceptionally long season of ornamental interest that spans from the emergence of the succulent, blue-green, glaucous foliage in spring through the broccoli-like flower bud stage in summer, peak bloom in late summer, and the distinctive russet-bronze dried seedhead stage in fall and winter that persists ornamentally into December. The flower clusters open dusty-pink in late August, deepen to rose-salmon through September, and then to brick-red to mahogany in October — a three-month color progression from a single set of flower heads. The dried, rust-colored seedheads persist attractively through winter and are outstanding in dried arrangements. Autumn Joy is extraordinarily adaptable — it thrives in sand, clay (if not waterlogged), drought, heat, coastal salt spray, and alkaline conditions that would challenge most perennials. In zone 9, the heat and humidity of the Gulf South are the primary challenges: Autumn Joy performs well but requires sharply drained soil and should not be planted where summer irrigation keeps soil consistently moist. The succulent stems and leaves store water efficiently, making established plants remarkably drought-tolerant. Divide every 3–4 years in spring as clumps enlarge.

common_name: May Night Salvia (Deep Indigo Hardy Meadow Sage)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, average soil in full sun; May Night is the Perennial Plant of the Year 1997 — recognized for its outstanding vigor, extended bloom season, and versatility in well-drained perennial borders.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, average soil in full sun; May Night (Mainacht) is the Perennial Plant of the Year 1997 and one of the most celebrated hardy perennial salvias — producing exceptionally dense, upright spikes of deep indigo-violet flowers with contrasting purple calyces that intensify the color even after the petals drop. The very deep, saturated blue-violet color (deeper and more blue than most salvias, approaching true indigo) in tight, dense whorls along upright spikes creates a striking vertical accent effect in the mid-border from late May through June, with strong rebloom when sheared to 6 inches after the first flush. The reblooming quality — producing two to three flower flushes per season when deadheaded — extends the ornamental value of May Night through summer and into fall. The aromatic, gray-green foliage is attractive when the plant is not in flower. May Night is an exceptional pollinator plant: bees of all species, hummingbirds, and butterflies feed heavily on the flowers throughout the bloom period. In zone 9a and warmer, May Night may perform as a short-lived perennial rather than a permanent one — the heat and humidity of the Gulf South challenge this plant most severely in its second and third years. Plant in excellent drainage, treat as an annual if it declines in summer, and consider it a reliable cool-season perennial in the milder portions of zone 8.

common_name: Pow Wow White Coneflower (Compact White-Rayed Coneflower)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean soil in full sun; Pow Wow White is more compact than standard Echinacea purpurea cultivars (12–20 inches vs. 24–36 inches), making it proportionate for smaller residential borders and container use.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean soil in full sun; Pow Wow White is a compact white coneflower selection — bred as part of the Pow Wow series to produce white-rayed flowers with a prominent golden-orange central cone on a plant significantly shorter and more tidy than standard Echinacea purpurea cultivars (12–20 inches compared to the 24–36 inches typical of older selections). The pure white ray petals (held more horizontally than the drooping rays of the species) radiate around the prominent, dome-shaped, golden-orange to bronze cone, creating a classic daisy-form with high visual impact at eye level in a smaller planting space. Pow Wow White provides excellent contrast in mixed perennial plantings when combined with purple or blue companions like Walker's Low catmint, Salvia May Night, or standard purple Echinacea. Like all Echinacea purpurea cultivars, Pow Wow White is a North American native prairie plant that is exceptionally drought-tolerant, heat-resistant, and low-maintenance once established. The flower heads attract goldfinches, chickadees, and other seed-eating birds through fall and winter — leaving the dried seedheads standing through winter provides wildlife habitat value while also providing ornamental winter interest. In zone 9, Echinacea performs reliably as a long-lived perennial; it is one of the most adaptable and drought-tolerant perennials available for the Gulf South border.
